title: SCORE — Modeler / Senior Modeler Assessment
description: Purely HR/behavioral instrument, 30 questions at ~60 seconds each. Technical/software skill is assessed separately (technical test + Personal Interview), so this test carries no technical items at all. Weighted for an individual-contributor role: Team Play, Communication, and Problem-Solving get the most questions; Leadership is present at a lighter weight to spot early potential; Dispute Resolution, Organizational Mindset, and Ethics carry small dedicated quotas but ethics-concern flags can surface on any question, not just the dedicated ones.

sections.instructions: Choose the option that best reflects how you would actually act. There is no single 'correct' answer — pick the one truest to your instinct.

questions.prompt: A new teammate joins mid-project, unfamiliar with your studio's model standards, while you're on a tight deadline yourself. What do you do?

options.text: Point them to the standards doc and let them figure it out — you don't have time.
Take 15-20 minutes to walk them through the essentials now, then check in with them later.
Ask someone else on the team to onboard them instead.
Ignore them until they ask you directly for help.

questions.prompt: A colleague on another discipline's team is overloaded and asks for informal help outside your assigned tasks. What do you do?

options.text: Decline — it's not your task list.
Help without limit even if it seriously risks your own deadline, without telling anyone.
Offer specific, bounded help and let your own lead know about the trade-off.
Offer to help 'later' without committing to anything concrete.

questions.prompt: In a team meeting, credit for a successful clash-resolution effort gets attributed mostly to someone who did less of the actual work. What do you do?

options.text: Say nothing — it's not worth the awkwardness.
Correct the record on the spot, emphasizing your own contribution assertively.
Mention the fuller picture calmly and factually, crediting everyone who contributed, including yourself.
Complain about it privately to other teammates afterward.

questions.prompt: You notice a teammate quietly struggling, and their slipping work is starting to threaten the team's shared deadline. What do you do?

options.text: Focus only on your own tasks — it's their responsibility.
Report their struggling to your manager immediately without talking to them first.
Check in with them directly and privately, understand what's going on, and offer specific support.
Quietly pick up their slack yourself without saying anything to them or anyone else.

questions.prompt: Your project wins client praise, but a teammate who contributed less publicly claims most of the credit in front of others. What's your reaction?

options.text: Publicly dispute their claim in the moment.
Let it go completely and never mention it, even though it bothers you.
Calmly and factually acknowledge the full team's contribution afterward, including your own, without making it confrontational.
Start doing the same thing yourself in future 'to even the score.'

questions.prompt: Helping another discipline hit their deadline would require you to slightly adjust your own schedule, at some real cost to you. What's your approach?

options.text: Refuse, since it's not your deadline to worry about.
Agree without asking any questions or flagging the cost to your own schedule.
Discuss the trade-off honestly with your lead and the other discipline, then agree to a workable compromise.
Agree reluctantly and let your resentment show throughout the interaction.

questions.prompt: You disagree with a decision your team made collectively in a meeting you weren't part of. What do you do?

options.text: Ignore the decision and do it your way anyway.
Comply outwardly but complain about it to others behind the scenes.
Raise your concern constructively with the team or decision-maker, then support the decision once discussed.
Go along with it without ever voicing your view, even in the moment.

questions.prompt: You need to explain a complex clash issue to non-technical stakeholders (e.g. the client's facilities team) who don't understand BIM jargon. What's your approach?

options.text: Use full technical terminology since it's the most precise.
Simplify heavily, using analogies and avoiding jargon, checking they understood before moving on.
Let a colleague handle the explanation instead.
Give a quick technical summary and move on regardless of whether they followed.

questions.prompt: An important coordination decision was made verbally in a meeting. What do you do to make sure nothing gets lost?

options.text: Assume everyone remembers and skip writing it down.
Write a brief but clear summary and share it with everyone involved right after the meeting.
Write detailed notes only for your own records, not shared with others.
Wait for someone else to write meeting minutes.

questions.prompt: A teammate's message about a coordination issue is unclear and could mean two very different things. What do you do?

options.text: Guess at the intended meaning and proceed.
Ask a clarifying question immediately before proceeding.
Proceed with the interpretation that's easiest for you, regardless of likely intent.
Ignore the message until they follow up.

questions.prompt: Partway through a task, you realize the brief you were given was ambiguous and you've made an assumption that might be wrong. What do you do?

options.text: Keep going and hope your assumption was right.
Reach out early to confirm your assumption before investing more time.
Finish the task your way and mention the ambiguity only if someone asks.
Redo the task multiple ways to cover all interpretations, without asking anyone.

questions.prompt: A client questions why a deliverable took longer than the timesheet estimate implied. What's the best response?

options.text: Say it was due to 'technical issues' without further detail.
Give a clear, honest breakdown of what the extra time involved and why, framed constructively.
Attribute the delay to a teammate.
Avoid answering directly and change the subject.

questions.prompt: Your manager's instructions for a task conflict with what the client previously told you directly. What do you do?

options.text: Follow your manager's instructions without mentioning the conflict.
Follow the client's version without mentioning the conflict to your manager.
Point out the discrepancy to your manager promptly so it can be clarified before proceeding.
Pick whichever seems easier to execute technically.

questions.prompt: Two disciplines' models disagree on a critical dimension by 200mm, discovered the day before an issue-for-construction deadline. What's your first move?

options.text: Pick whichever number looks right and proceed.
Escalate immediately with both models/screenshots to your lead, flagging it as blocking until jointly resolved.
Issue the set anyway with a note about the discrepancy.
Wait to see if someone else notices it first.

questions.prompt: You realize you misjudged the time a modeling task would take and are now behind, with the deadline in two days. What do you do?

options.text: Say nothing and work faster, skipping some QA checks to catch up.
Inform your lead early with a realistic revised estimate, and ask about reprioritizing scope or support.
Wait until the deadline to explain what happened.
Attribute the slip mainly to an unclear brief at the start.

questions.prompt: A software malfunction during a critical work session right before a deadline costs you an hour of unsaved work. What do you do?

options.text: Panic and immediately tell everyone the deadline is impossible.
Quickly assess what's actually lost, try alternate recovery options, and give a realistic revised estimate to your lead.
Try to silently redo the work without adjusting the estimate or informing anyone.
Blame the software publicly and wait for instructions.

questions.prompt: You're given conflicting information from two sources about a critical requirement (e.g. ceiling height) for the same space. What's your approach?

options.text: Pick the source that's more convenient for your existing work.
Cross-check both sources against the master documents/BEP and escalate if still unresolved.
Use whichever number was given most recently, regardless of source reliability.
Proceed with an average of the two numbers without verifying either.

questions.prompt: A task turns out to be more complex than expected, and you're not sure of the best technical approach. What do you do?

options.text: Guess and hope it works out.
Research standard approaches, and if still unsure, ask a more experienced colleague before committing significant time.
Pick the first approach that comes to mind and stick with it regardless of new information.
Avoid the task and hope someone else picks it up.

questions.prompt: You're assigned a task with insufficient information to complete it accurately, and the person who could clarify is unavailable for a day. The deadline is tomorrow. What do you do?

options.text: Guess and proceed with your best assumption, telling no one.
Make a reasonable, clearly-documented assumption, proceed cautiously on lower-risk parts first, and flag the exact open question as soon as the person is available.
Stop all work on the task until the person is available.
Ask a random colleague who also doesn't know the answer and treat their guess as fact.

questions.prompt: A newer teammate keeps making the same mistake, creating rework for you. Even though you're not their manager, what do you do?

options.text: Redo their work yourself each time to avoid confrontation.
Report it directly to management asking for them to be reprimanded.
Sit with them, explain the correct method, and check their next few submissions before escalating further.
Point out the mistake publicly in the coordination meeting so others are aware.

questions.prompt: During a chaotic coordination crunch, no one has clearly taken charge of triaging what to fix first. What do you do, even without formal authority?

options.text: Wait for someone senior to take charge.
Step in to suggest a simple triage/priority order for the group to align around, without waiting to be asked.
Focus only on your own piece and let the group figure out priorities.
Take over the whole thing yourself without checking with anyone.

questions.prompt: You disagree with a modeling standard your studio uses and believe your own method is better. What do you do?

options.text: Quietly use your own method since you're confident it's better.
Raise it with your lead/BIM manager with reasoning and evidence, following the current standard while it's discussed.
Complain to colleagues that the standard is outdated.
Follow the standard without ever raising the concern.

questions.prompt: A junior colleague asks you, an experienced modeler, whether they're ready to take on more responsibility. What do you do?

options.text: Give a quick generic answer and move on.
Take the time to give specific, honest feedback based on what you've actually observed of their work, and encourage a concrete next step.
Tell them to ask their manager instead.
Avoid giving an honest answer to spare their feelings.

questions.prompt: In a coordination meeting, a structural engineer insists a beam position is fixed and MEP must reroute around it — even though that reroute would violate minimum ceiling clearance required by code. What do you do?

options.text: Agree immediately to avoid conflict and reroute despite the code issue.
Push back forcefully insisting structural must move the beam, without proposing alternatives.
Clearly flag the code conflict with technical justification and propose 2-3 alternatives for the group to evaluate together.
Stay quiet in the meeting but raise the concern privately with your manager afterward.

questions.prompt: During a late-night coordination call under deadline pressure, a colleague is visibly stressed and starts snapping at others. What do you do?

options.text: Ignore it — not your problem.
Snap back to defend the person being criticized.
Acknowledge the stress calmly, refocus on the specific issue, and suggest a short break if needed.
Report the colleague's behavior to management right after the call without addressing it in the moment.

questions.prompt: Two teammates on your project openly disagree about the right modeling approach in front of you and ask you to weigh in. What do you do?

options.text: Avoid taking a side to stay neutral, even if one is clearly more correct.
Give your honest technical opinion based on the merits, while being respectful of both people's perspectives.
Side with whichever one is more senior, regardless of the merits.
Escalate it immediately without offering your own view first.

questions.prompt: A recurring coordination issue keeps causing delays across multiple projects, not just yours. What's the best individual response as a modeler (not yet a manager)?

options.text: Keep solving it manually every time it comes up on your own projects.
Document the recurring pattern and its impact, and proactively suggest it to your lead as a process issue worth fixing at the source.
Assume it's someone else's job to notice and say nothing.
Complain about it informally without ever raising it formally.

questions.prompt: Your studio is under resourcing pressure across several projects at once, and you finish your own task early. What do you do?

options.text: Consider your job done and wait for your next assigned task.
Proactively check with your lead whether another project or teammate needs help, given the studio-wide crunch.
Use the spare time entirely for personal tasks without telling anyone.
Wait to be told what to do next without asking.

questions.prompt: At 6pm you discover an error in the shared central model that will affect three other disciplines' work if not fixed tonight. Fixing it means at least 2 unplanned overtime hours. What do you do?

options.text: Log off and mention it in tomorrow's meeting since you weren't asked to stay late.
Fix it tonight yourself, but don't log the extra hours so it doesn't look like you're padding your timesheet.
Notify your coordinator immediately about the issue and its downstream impact, offer to fix it tonight, and log the actual time honestly.
Fix it tonight but don't mention it unless someone asks.

questions.prompt: Your manager asks you to sign off on a coordination model as 'clash-free,' but you know a few unresolved clashes remain in a low-visibility area. What do you do?

options.text: Sign off since your manager asked.
Decline to sign off and clearly document the remaining clashes and their risk before any decision is made.
Sign off but quietly tell a colleague about the unresolved clashes.
Delay responding and hope someone else handles it.
